category: Liquidity crisis from concentrated wholesale funding
risk_signals: Over 60% of funding came from short-term wholesale markets
Loan book grew faster than deposits for eight consecutive quarters
Single-name exposure exceeded 25% of Tier 1 capital
analyst_reasoning: A bank that funds long-dated loans with short-term wholesale money is exposed to a sudden loss of market confidence. When growth is this fast, underwriting standards usually slip, so the funding mismatch and the concentration compound each other rather than adding up linearly.
investigation_steps: Reconstruct the funding maturity ladder to size the roll-over cliff
Stress the largest single-name exposures against a 30% collateral haircut
Compare the loan-loss provisioning trend against peer banks
decision: The risk committee capped wholesale funding at 40% and froze single-name limits, but only after the regulator flagged the mismatch.
outcome: A brief market panic triggered a funding run. The bank survived only after an emergency central-bank liquidity line, and its share price fell roughly 70%.
lessons_learned: Funding concentration is a liquidity risk even when the underlying assets are sound
Rapid asset growth is itself a warning sign, not a success metric
Concentration limits imposed only after a regulator flags them arrive too late
